speaker: interviewer
text: May 3rd, 1996. The survivor is Benjamin Bender. You were telling us about your life in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The life in ghetto was very harsh. We have rationed food. Living in dilapidated housing. No plumbing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The young, who are trying to obtain life certificates, you know, like to work in certain places. The old couldn't do it for obvious reasons. Because you-- when you are 50 or 45, you couldn't go to into a any German office and ask for-- for a job. From time to time, the German entered the ghetto, rounding up people just at random, and taking them away for execution, or just sending them away to Treblinka, or to other places.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I mean the number of the Jews were diminishing constantly. But some were trying to hide. And-- but the life was very, I wouldn't say dull, but it was a life when you live in certain fear. And you live in terror. When you expect something to happen-- something like a miracle, or a quick end to the war, which that never happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the atmosphere in-- in the ghetto. Total despair. People were depressed, who were meeting each other you know, sad, and knowing that something awful will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- was there illness in the ghetto? Did you have doctors or hospitals set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was also a big problem, because we knew [INAUDIBLE] there were sick people. But I would say there were less sick people-- I don't recall-- I mean-- my neighbor, or my friends, or my relatives being sick. Because if you went into the hospital, the German liquidated the hospital when they went into the hospital, and they took the sick people away. So people are very leery to go to the hospital. So they tried to stay home. There were doctors. There were doctors. Not too many. Because they eliminated a lot of doctors when they invaded the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't were there any schools set up in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, as I said before, I was very thirsty for knowledge. I was eager to learn. And there was an underground school formed by three or four former teachers. Were a group of about maybe 10 or 12 girls and then boys. And I was attending this underground school, which was kind of a conspiracy. You are trying to preserve sanity among insanity. Waiting for the day of awakening, when the war will come to an end. And we again normal people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll go back to where we had left off, after the beginning of the Final Solution. And you had run this distance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [COUGHS] We reached this place. People were exhausted. Some passed out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on top of everything, one of the Lithuanians, he looked at me. I don't know why. He picked me up, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and said to me, I have to do 25 push-ups. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was lucky that he picked me up for this purpose, because if he would ask any other, they couldn't do it. They could do maybe one push-up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, look at me. I couldn't understand why. I couldn't ask the question, why me? He looked at me and he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you have to count when you do the push-up. If you miss one push-up, and you have an error in your counting, you'll get another 25. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I did the 25 push-ups in a short time. And he looked at me and he was astonished. And he said in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. This means you are done. I was working at a construction site. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't have money, because I was naive. I just left everything home, because the German said all things of value should be left. [COUGHS] And I-- I could buy bread if I would have money. But I didn't have. After about a couple weeks, there was another selection. We are being led to another street-- Krotka street, which is Krotka. That means [INAUDIBLE]. K-R-O-- the Polish O has a little point on the top, which makes it O-T-K-A. Krotka. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then selection. We were-- we had to form columns, five, five, five, five. There I saw Captain Degenhardt, [COUGHS] the butcher of Czestochowa. Next to him, his bodyguard. Professional killer; trigger happy. Using his left hand, for his revolver. Killing by killing-- shooting into the left eye of the person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before me was a boy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe eight years old-- very short. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He was behind me, sorry. Then he said to me, please do me a favor. Can you change places? You will be at the end, and I will be before you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I didn't-- I ask him a question why. Because I am too short. And he had a small valise, small trunk. He put the trunk before me. And he was standing on the trunk. He was almost reaching my-- my height. Next to me stood a man maybe 6' 2', 6' 4'. A giant Jew. Then Degenhardt was pacing with his swagger stick, and pointing from time to time at a person. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When he reached my column, he pointed his swagger stick at me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I was paralyzed. But I stepped out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I did it, I heard a sentence which he said to his bodyguard. It was in German. I understood German. I re-said it in Polish. Because they say in German, in German [SPEAKING GERMAN]. 'What is doing the woman between, among, the men?'

speaker: interviewee
text: It was I had blonde hair, curly blond hairs. Bushy hairs. And he saw probably the top of my head. He didn't see my-- my face. When he looked at me, and he burst-- he was laughing. And of course he was laughing. His bodyguard was laughing, too. And they told me to step on into the column. In a matter of seconds, my life came to a standstill. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sure that he [INAUDIBLE] didn't like me, or-- but he didn't say nothing. Then the next guy next to me, he'd have to look up to him. He was maybe 5' 6', 5' 5'. And the guy was 6' 2'. The Jews were being portrayed as ugly creatures-- subhuman. In films, in newspapers, you could see pictures of ugly creatures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he asked him, what's your profession? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The guy said, I have two profession. I am a carpenter, and I am a plumber. He looked at a him, you know. He was smiling. He said to him, you are overqualified. And he sent him to Treblinka. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after this selection, we went back to my old working place. But then I have to mention an important point. My parents were working at the bath house, which was outside the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because this was a bath house, it was outside the ghetto. So about 12 Jews-- doctor, an engineer, and some working people-- were allowed to work in this place, under the special permission of the German authorities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I went to the-- to take a shower-- every week I used to go-- I met my parents. And then I escaped after the shower. I didn't join my column [INAUDIBLE]. And the guy who was Lithuanian, he remembered me very well. And my parents were living on the attic, very top, top, small room-- which were two rooms. One room, a closet. Was a door in the closet, and a removable board in the back to another room. I hid myself into this room. Meanwhile, the Lithuanian was searching for the missing guy. And he was berserk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he didn't find me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was already 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, beginning. Beginning, yes. Then I was hidden in this place. Without a permit to be a part of this [NON-ENGLISH]. Nobody was aware of this, that I was in this place. I was in the second little room, with a kerosene lamp surrounded by hundreds of books, a property of a doctor who was sent to Treblinka. Was living in-- in this place, or maybe he got only a storage room. Whatever. I recall I was reading every book-- even without understanding. It was in Polish. With a book of [NON-ENGLISH] Stefan Zweig; of German philosopher Leibniz, Kant, Schopenhauer. Henrich Heine. Wolfgang Goethe. Friedrich Christian Schiller. The giants. The good sons of a different Germany. There was a broken mirror on the floor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now I know reading the book, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was testing myself In the mirror. As I said before, still believing, preparing myself for the big awakening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which never happened at that time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The doctor was in charge of the building. Was a former Polish officer, a doctor. He was more [? poor ?] than a Jew. He spoke fluently German. He was a good friend of Captain Degenhardt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he liked him because he was just a [INAUDIBLE] man. One day, or one night, it was June or July. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Dr. Wahlberg got an order [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by a Polish cop to go for interview, for a meeting with Captain Degenhardt. It was dusk. I was in the little window looking into the street, which was totally deserted. The headquarters of the SS was in the street at the corner of Garibaldi-- this was the street-- and Wilson-- Wilson street. And then, after 15 minutes, there was a shot [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] resounding in the street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Something very unusual, because the street was empty, deserted. There was a big warehouse for the Jewish property. Furniture, all kind of clothing, shoes. Then after 15 minutes, a Polish cop came running. My father opened the gate. He went down. His wife, the doctor's wife, and his daughter, age 16, were living in this place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said to her that her husband was killed by [? Willy ?] [? Unclebach ?]. Degenhardt wasn't even present, he was in Krakow for a meeting with a Governor Frank-- F-R-A-N-K, Frank-- who was the governor of Krakow. And he took them. And he transferred them, as far as we know, alive, to the Aryan part of Czestochowa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened to them, I have no-- no idea. I should mention to you that we built a bunker in this bath house. We did it at night-- our hammers wrapped in rags. There shouldn't be any sound. We built a double wall, which was connected with the outside waste bin. In Poland, there were no barrels like we have here, or wastebin. It was a cement, a huge wastebin. And the entrance was through this wastebin to the bunker. When the next day the remnants of the people from this bathhouse left, my parents and myself, we stayed In the bathhouse. Still, workers were coming for a shower. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then my father, the last group came evening. It was-- that I was getting dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said to me, you have to leave us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I say why? I was building this. I was naive. I was 14, 15. I want to stay with you. I love you. You are my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember my mother was crying. She was speechless. My father was more composed. Even-- he was devastated. And I was pleading. And he said, we are sorry, you have to leave this place. You cannot stay with us. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It didn't help me much. I felt being rejected. I couldn't understand what they had in mind. Why they were so despondent, and why they thought, I mean, I don't belong to them anymore. I left the group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw my father standing outside. I think he was crying. And I was walking. I was looking back, another step, another step, another step. And he disappeared. This was the last time I saw him. Later, not from my brother-- who knew the secret-- but after the war, in a kibbutz I learned. I met a man who was working at the Garibaldi street, cleaning the building. And he told me they had committed suicide by taking cyanide. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I left the bath house, I went straight to HASAG. This was the ammunition factory. it was a big-- it was almost a town, with 5,000 working people. My brother was working in this place. He was working this place because I was hidden in this attic. So he was working in this place. So I joined him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if he knew that my parents-- my parents were dead. But then he was disappearing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every time. There were a group of Jews who were praying. And then I learned that later, not from him-- from somebody else-- that he was saying Kaddish for my parents. I didn't know that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The life in HASAG, it was a hard labor camp. It was harsh, but bearable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was strong. We got our soup, piece of bread; sometimes a piece of margarine-- and sugar, which was unusual. But because it was a ammunition factory, and they had a big output of ammunition, and they were satisfied with the working Jews, they gave us somehow, special treatment. But the selection were continuing-- not by the SS-- just by the Meisters, the supervisors who are taking care of the working group. We, two of us, we are like two hobos. We have friends in this place. We have some relatives-- distant relatives-- who were good off-- good off, in a way. They have money. They have clothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They didn't look at us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then later, I-- I will skip. When I came to Buchenwald, and I saw the inscription on the gate-- 'Jedem das Seine.' Everybody for himself. Then I understood that people, even close to each other-- cousins, second cousins, kind of relatives, good friends-- everybody was for himself. I think this total rejection, had a tremendous impact on my life. Even today, over 50 years after being a free man, the shadow of this total rejection is shadowing my life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We managed. My brother was very pragmatic, very intelligent, very bright. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a dreamer, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] seeing myself in a better world, and believing in survival. But you have to survive. You have to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he did something which I never understood. He tried to-- he was in touch with some Poles, like the women who are working in this factory. So he used to get some onions, or from time to time, a piece of bread. He did something which he never told me, but we got some little more food.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one German, a former Wehrmacht officer. He was half paralyzed. He liked my brother. My brother knew fluently German.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to take a tape break now.
